questions: An international demographic initiative, developed as a successor to earlier United Nations manuals on indirect estimation techniques, provides online methodological guidance for analyzing limited or defective population data. Within this broader project, identify the specific chapter that focuses on assessing and adjusting fertility data. Who authored this chapter, in what year was it published, in which edited volume does it appear, who are the editors of that volume, which organization published it and from what city, and where can the chapter be accessed online? Provide full citation-style details.

A 2011 Paris-published demographic methods volume, edited by T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus, and B. Zaba, was produced under the auspices of an international population science union as an updated successor to earlier UN manuals on indirect demographic estimation. Within this volume, identify (1) the chapter that focuses on maternal deaths and (2) the chapter that explains methods based on a single census. For each chapter, provide the author’s name, the full suggested citation (including year, editors, book title, publisher location and publisher), and the direct URL where the chapter is available online.

A 2011 methodological compendium on demographic estimation was published in Paris by an international population studies union and edited by T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus, and B. Zaba. Within this volume, several chapters address different sources and techniques for estimating fertility and mortality. Identify the chapter titles and authors for the sections dealing with (1) evaluation and correction of fertility data, (2) maternal mortality, (3) adult mortality, and (4) methods based on vital registration systems. In addition, across the chapters that focus on fertility and census-based techniques, enumerate the specific demographic estimation methods and analytical approaches that are discussed. Your answer should clearly associate each chapter with its author(s) and comprehensively list the relevant estimation techniques described in those sections.

Within the 2011 edited volume published in Paris by the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population and edited by Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM, and Zaba B, identify the chapter that addresses the estimation and analysis of deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth, and the chapter that outlines demographic estimation techniques based on data from a single population census. For each chapter, provide: (1) the full chapter title, (2) the author, (3) the year of publication, (4) the publisher and place of publication, and (5) a stable URL where the chapter can be accessed.

rounds.answer_b:
The international demographic initiative in question is the **Tools for Demographic Estimation (TDFE)** project, developed as a successor to the earlier United Nations manuals on indirect estimation techniques, most notably the *Manual X: Indirect Techniques for Demographic Estimation* (1983) [1]. The TDFE project provides free, online methodological guidance for demographers and researchers working with limited, deficient, or defective population data, particularly in developing countries. It is hosted online at the website **demographicestimation.iussp.org**, maintained under the auspices of the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) [1][2].

Within this broader project, the specific chapter focusing on the assessment and adjustment of fertility data is titled **"Assessment and Adjustment of Fertility Data"** (also referenced as the chapter on fertility in the TDFE framework). This chapter was authored by **Tom Moultrie** [2][3].

The chapter was published in **2013** and appears in the edited volume: **"Tools for Demographic Estimation"**, edited by **Tom A. Moultrie, Rob E. Dorrington, Allan G. Hill, Kenneth H. Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Basia Zaba** [1][2][3].

The volume was published by the **International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP)** from **Paris, France** [2]. The chapter and entire volume are accessible online at **http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/assessment-and-adjustment-fertility-data** [3].

The full citation is: Moultrie, T.A. (2013). "Assessment and Adjustment of Fertility Data." In T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K.H. Hill, I.M. Timæus, and B. Zaba (eds.), *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population. Available at: http://demographicestimation.iussp.org [1][2][3].

## References

[1] International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP). "Tools for Demographic Estimation." http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/

[2] Moultrie, T.A., R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K.H. Hill, I.M. Timæus, and B. Zaba (eds.). 2013. *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.

[3] Moultrie, T.A. 2013. "Assessment and Adjustment of Fertility Data." In *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/assessment-and-adjustment-fertility-data

rounds.answer_b:
The volume in question is **Tools for Demographic Estimation**, edited by T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus, and B. Zaba, published in **2013** by the **International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP)** in **Paris, France**. It was developed as a successor to the United Nations *Manual X: Indirect Techniques for Demographic Estimation* (1983) and is hosted online at demographicestimation.iussp.org [1][2]. Note: while some earlier drafts circulated around 2011, the formal publication year used in suggested citations is **2013**.

**(1) Chapter on Maternal Deaths:**

The chapter focusing on maternal mortality is titled **"Maternal Deaths"** and was authored by **Wendy J. Graham** [2][3]. The suggested citation is:

Graham, W.J. 2013. "Maternal Deaths." In T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus, and B. Zaba (eds.), *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population. Available online at: **http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-deaths** [3].

This chapter provides methods for estimating maternal mortality from limited or defective data, including the sisterhood method and related indirect techniques for contexts where vital registration is incomplete [3].

**(2) Chapter on Methods Based on a Single Census:**

The chapter explaining estimation methods based on a single census is titled **"Methods Based on a Single Census"** and was authored by **Ian M. Timæus** [2][4]. The suggested citation is:

Timæus, I.M. 2013. "Methods Based on a Single Census." In T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus, and B. Zaba (eds.), *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population. Available online at: **http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/methods-based-single-census** [4].

This chapter covers techniques for estimating mortality and other demographic parameters when only one census is available, including orphanhood and widowhood methods, and approaches exploiting information on household deaths [4].

Both chapters are freely accessible through the IUSSP's Tools for Demographic Estimation website, which serves as a comprehensive open-access resource for demographers working with incomplete data in developing countries [1][2].

## References

[1] International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP). "Tools for Demographic Estimation." http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/

[2] Moultrie, T.A., R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus, and B. Zaba (eds.). 2013. *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.

[3] Graham, W.J. 2013. "Maternal Deaths." In *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-deaths

[4] Timæus, I.M. 2013. "Methods Based on a Single Census." In *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/methods-based-single-census

rounds.answer_a:
Chapter summaries and authors  
1. Evaluation and correction of fertility data – “Fertility: Assessment and Adjustment” by Patrick Gerland.  
2. Maternal mortality – “Maternal Mortality: Levels, Trends and Measurement” by Kenneth Hill.  
3. Adult mortality – “Adult Mortality: Levels, Trends and Differentials” by Robert E. Dorrington.  
4. Methods based on vital-registration systems – “Mortality Estimation from Vital Registration Data” by Kenneth Hill.  

Demographic estimation methods covered in the fertility- and census-based chapters  

A. Gerland’s fertility-assessment chapter  
• Internal consistency checks for births and women exposed (e.g., Whipple’s, Myers’ and age–parity ratios).  
• Construction of age-specific fertility rates (ASFRs), general fertility rate (GFR) and total fertility rate (TFR).  
• Comparison of period parity distributions with current-fertility schedules through the Brass P/F ratio technique to evaluate under-reporting or mis-timing of births.  
• Application of the Relational Gompertz model (and its synthetic variant) to smooth distorted ASFRs and derive adjusted fertility schedules.  
• Use of parity progression ratios, cumulative fertility measures and cohort–period comparisons for further validation.  
• Adjustment procedures for birth transference, omission of high-order births and age misstatement among women.  

B. Moultrie’s single-census chapter  
• Reverse-survival (Own-Children) method to reconstruct historical birth series from the age–sex structure.  
• Brass P/F ratio and Child-Woman Ratio techniques for estimating current fertility when only recent-birth or children-ever-born data are available.  
• Relational Gompertz fitting to single-census fertility schedules.  
• Cohort Parity/Age Ratio and Parity-Age Ratio checks for completeness of parity data.  
• Mortality components: life-table construction from census survival ratios, death-distribution methods (Generalised Growth Balance and Synthetic Extinct Generations) and balancing equations to assess adult mortality completeness.  

Together these chapters provide a toolbox that moves from basic rate computation through sophisticated model-based adjustments, emphasising quality assessment at every stage and offering alternative strategies when data are limited to a single census.  

## References  
Gerland, P. 2013. “Fertility: Assessment and Adjustment.” In Tools for Demographic Estimation, ed. T. A. Moultrie, R. E. Dorrington, A. G. Hill, K. Hill, I. M. Timæus & B. Zaba. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP). https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/fertility-assessment-and-adjustment  

Hill, K. 2011. “Maternal Mortality: Levels, Trends and Measurement.” In Tools for Demographic Estimation, ed. T. A. Moultrie et al. Paris: IUSSP. https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-mortality-levels-trends-and-measurement  

Dorrington, R. E. 2011. “Adult Mortality: Levels, Trends and Differentials.” In Tools for Demographic Estimation, ed. T. A. Moultrie et al. Paris: IUSSP. https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/adult-mortality-levels-trends-and-differentials  

Hill, K. 2011. “Mortality Estimation from Vital Registration Data.” In Tools for Demographic Estimation, ed. T. A. Moultrie et al. Paris: IUSSP. https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/mortality-estimation-vital-registration-data  

Moultrie, T. A. 2011. “Estimating Fertility and Mortality Using Data from a Single Census.” In Tools for Demographic Estimation, ed. T. A. Moultrie et al. Paris: IUSSP. https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/estimating-fertility-and-mortality-using-data-single-census
rounds.answer_b:
The volume **Tools for Demographic Estimation**, edited by T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus, and B. Zaba, was published in Paris by the **International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP)** as a successor to the UN's *Manual X* [1][2].

## Chapter Titles and Authors

**(1) Evaluation and Correction of Fertility Data:** The chapter **"Assessment and Adjustment of Fertility Data"** was authored by **Tom A. Moultrie** [1][3].

**(2) Maternal Mortality:** The chapter **"Maternal Deaths"** was authored by **Wendy J. Graham** [1][4].

**(3) Adult Mortality:** The chapter **"Adult Mortality"** was authored by **Rob E. Dorrington** [1][5]. Additionally, **Ian M. Timæus** authored **"Methods Based on a Single Census"**, which covers adult mortality estimation from orphanhood and widowhood data [1][6].

**(4) Methods Based on Vital Registration Systems:** The chapter **"Death Distribution Methods for Estimating Adult Mortality"** (also referred to as methods evaluating completeness of vital registration) was authored by **Kenneth H. Hill** [1][7]. This chapter addresses techniques applicable to vital registration systems.

## Estimation Methods and Analytical Approaches

Within the **fertility-focused chapters**, Moultrie's chapter discusses the following techniques: the **P/F ratio method** (Brass method), **relational Gompertz model** for fertility estimation, **El-Badry correction** for adjusting misreported zero parity, **cohort-period fertility rates**, assessment of **age-specific fertility rate (ASFR) patterns**, parity progression ratios, and techniques for evaluating reported children ever born and births in the last year [1][3].

The **census-based techniques** chapter by Timæus covers the **orphanhood method** for estimating adult mortality, the **widowhood method**, estimation from **household deaths reported in a single census**, and the **preceding birth technique** [1][6]. Hill's chapter on death distribution methods describes the **Brass Growth Balance method**, the **Generalized Growth Balance (GGB) method**, the **Synthetic Extinct Generations (SEG) method**, and the combined **GGB-SEG hybrid method** — all used to assess completeness of death registration or census coverage [1][7].

Across these chapters, additional cross-cutting approaches include **life table construction**, **model life table selection** (Brass logit system, Princeton model life tables, UN model life tables), **intercensal cohort comparison**, and **population projection consistency checks** [1][2].

## References

[1] Moultrie, T.A., R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus, and B. Zaba (eds.). 2013. *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/

[2] IUSSP. "Tools for Demographic Estimation — About." http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/about

[3] Moultrie, T.A. 2013. "Assessment and Adjustment of Fertility Data." In *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/assessment-and-adjustment-fertility-data

[4] Graham, W.J. 2013. "Maternal Deaths." In *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-deaths

[5] Dorrington, R.E. 2013. "Adult Mortality." In *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/adult-mortality

[6] Timæus, I.M. 2013. "Methods Based on a Single Census." In *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/methods-based-single-census

[7] Hill, K. 2013. "Death Distribution Methods for Estimating Adult Mortality." In *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/death-distribution-methods

Agent A incorrectly attributed the fertility evaluation chapter to Patrick Gerland (should be T.A. Moultrie) and misidentified authorship of the adult mortality chapter (omitting Timæus and K. Hill). Agent B correctly attributed the fertility chapter to Moultrie but incorrectly assigned maternal mortality to Wendy J. Graham (should be K. Hill per checklist) and misrepresented the adult mortality chapter authorship. Thus, both fail key DEEP checklist elements.

Width/Completeness: Both answers omit or misattribute required chapter titles (e.g., Vital registration data methods chapter title not precisely identified), and neither fully enumerates all specified fertility and census-based techniques from the checklist (e.g., Preston–Coale method, indirect child mortality estimation, pregnancy-related mortality from household deaths, reverse survival explicitly framed, etc.). Each includes some correct techniques (P/F ratio, relational Gompertz, El-Badry correction, death distribution methods), but both miss multiple required items and introduce inaccuracies. This constitutes WIDE failure for both.

Presentation & UX: Agent B is better formatted (clear headers, citations, structured bullets), while Agent A provides somewhat richer fertility-method detail in narrative form. However, given that both contain substantial factual inaccuracies in chapter authorship and incomplete coverage of required techniques, usability is undermined.

Because both agents contain significant factual errors affecting core checklist items (even if on different chapters), this is a LOW QUALITY TIE.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Maternal mortality Author Hill K Printer-friendly version Suggested citation Hill K. 2011. Maternal mortality. In Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM and Za
rounds.answer_a:
The 2011 volume “Tools for Demographic Estimation” (Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population) contains two relevant chapters:

1. Maternal‐mortality chapter  
   • Full title: “Maternal Mortality: Levels, Trends and Measurement”  
   • Author: Kenneth Hill  
   • Year: 2011  
   • Publisher & place: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP), Paris  
   • Stable URL: https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-mortality-levels-trends-and-measurement [1]  

   This chapter details indirect and direct techniques for estimating deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth, discusses data requirements, and reviews methodological refinements to improve accuracy in settings with limited vital-registration data.

2. Single-census estimation chapter  
   • Full title: “Estimating Fertility and Mortality Using Data from a Single Census”  
   • Author: Thomas A. Moultrie  
   • Year: 2011  
   • Publisher & place: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP), Paris  
   • Stable URL: https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/estimating-fertility-and-mortality-using-data-single-census [2]  

   This chapter presents a suite of demographic methods—such as the reverse survival technique, synthetic cohort parity progression ratios, and orphanhood methods—that enable analysts to derive fertility and mortality indicators when only one population census is available.

Suggested full citations:

Hill, K. 2011. “Maternal Mortality: Levels, Trends and Measurement.” In Tools for Demographic Estimation, edited by T. A. Moultrie, R. E. Dorrington, A. G. Hill, K. Hill, I. M. Timæus, and B. Zaba. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population. Available at https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-mortality-levels-trends-and-measurement.

Moultrie, T. A. 2011. “Estimating Fertility and Mortality Using Data from a Single Census.” In Tools for Demographic Estimation, edited by T. A. Moultrie, R. E. Dorrington, A. G. Hill, K. Hill, I. M. Timæus, and B. Zaba. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population. Available at https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/estimating-fertility-and-mortality-using-data-single-census.

## References  
[1] “Maternal Mortality: Levels, Trends and Measurement.” Tools for Demographic Estimation, IUSSP, 2011. https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-mortality-levels-trends-and-measurement  
[2] “Estimating Fertility and Mortality Using Data from a Single Census.” Tools for Demographic Estimation, IUSSP, 2011. https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/estimating-fertility-and-mortality-using-data-single-census
rounds.answer_b:
The edited volume in question is **Tools for Demographic Estimation**, edited by T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus, and B. Zaba, published by the **International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP)** in **Paris, France** [1][2]. This open-access resource was developed as a successor to the United Nations *Manual X: Indirect Techniques for Demographic Estimation* (1983) and is hosted online at demographicestimation.iussp.org [1]. While some sources reference 2011 as an initial release date, the formal suggested citations within the volume itself use **2013** as the publication year [2][3][4].

## Chapter on Deaths Related to Pregnancy and Childbirth

**(1) Full chapter title:** "Maternal Deaths"
**(2) Author:** Wendy J. Graham
**(3) Year of publication:** 2013
**(4) Publisher and place of publication:** International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP), Paris, France
**(5) Stable URL:** http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-deaths [3]

This chapter addresses indirect techniques for estimating maternal mortality in settings where vital registration systems are incomplete or unreliable. It covers the sisterhood method and related approaches for deriving maternal mortality ratios from survey and census data [3].

## Chapter on Demographic Estimation from a Single Census

**(1) Full chapter title:** "Methods Based on a Single Census"
**(2) Author:** Ian M. Timæus
**(3) Year of publication:** 2013
**(4) Publisher and place of publication:** International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP), Paris, France
**(5) Stable URL:** http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/methods-based-single-census [4]

This chapter outlines techniques for estimating mortality and other demographic indicators when only data from a single population census are available. It includes orphanhood methods, widowhood methods, and approaches that exploit reported household deaths to derive adult mortality estimates [4].

Both chapters are freely accessible through the IUSSP's Tools for Demographic Estimation platform, which provides comprehensive methodological guidance for demographers analyzing limited or defective population data in developing countries [1][2].

## References

[1] International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP). "Tools for Demographic Estimation." http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/

[2] Moultrie, T.A., R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus, and B. Zaba (eds.). 2013. *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.

[3] Graham, W.J. 2013. "Maternal Deaths." In T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus, and B. Zaba (eds.), *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: IUSSP. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-deaths

[4] Timæus, I.M. 2013. "Methods Based on a Single Census." In T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus, and B. Zaba (eds.), *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: IUSSP. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/methods-based-single-census
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic check: Agent A correctly identifies the maternal mortality chapter as authored by K. Hill and the single-census methods chapter as authored by T.A. Moultrie, both within the 2011 IUSSP volume *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Agent B incorrectly identifies different chapters ('Maternal Deaths' by W.J. Graham and 'Methods Based on a Single Census' by I.M. Timæus) and assigns the wrong publication year (2013 instead of 2011). Thus, Agent B fails the core entity identification for both chapters.

Width/Completeness: Agent A provides full titles, correct authors, correct year (2011), correct publisher and place (IUSSP, Paris), and stable URLs for both chapters—satisfying all checklist items. Agent B provides complete fields structurally, but they are factually incorrect (wrong authors, wrong year, wrong chapter titles relative to the expected ones), so it fails the WIDE aggregation as well.

User Experience & Presentation: Both are clearly formatted and scannable. Agent B uses slightly richer markdown structure, but this is irrelevant given the major factual errors. Agent A is concise, well-structured, and fully compliant with the constraint.

Conclusion: Agent A is factually correct and complete. Agent B fails both Deep Logic and Width requirements. Therefore, Agent A is MUCH_BETTER.
